past_medical_history: Febrile seizures in childhood (ages 18 months and 3 years)
History of mild head injury from a bicycle accident at age 12, no loss of consciousness
family_history: Mother with history of febrile seizures in childhood
Maternal uncle diagnosed with epilepsy, well-controlled on medication
Father with hypertension and type 2 diabetes
No family history of brain tumors or neurodegenerative disease

social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and one child (age 4) in a single-family home
social_history.driving: Holds a valid driver's license, drives daily for errands

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent without paraphasic errors. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Short-term recall is 3/3 at five minutes. Mildly fatigued in appearance, consistent with post-ictal recovery approximately 6 hours prior.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally (3 mm to 2 mm). Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch in all three divisions of CN V bilaterally. Facial movements symmetric. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal in all extremities. Strength 5/5 throughout upper and lower extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift. No involuntary movements observed.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, proprioception, and vibration intact in all extremities. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing performed without dysmetria bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait steady with normal base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg sign negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue bite observed. Patient reports mild right-sided tongue soreness from a lateral tongue bite that occurred during the witnessed generalized seizure.

patient.chief_complaint: First-time witnessed generalized seizure preceded by a strange rising sensation in the stomach and a feeling of deja vu
patient.history_present_illness:
Mr. Hernandez is a 34-year-old right-handed male software developer who presents to the emergency department after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home. His wife reports that approximately six hours prior to arrival, she observed the patient suddenly stop mid-conversation and stare blankly for about 10 to 15 seconds. He appeared to smack his lips and fumble with his shirt buttons with his right hand. She called his name but he did not respond. He then developed stiffening of all four extremities followed by rhythmic bilateral jerking lasting approximately 90 seconds. He was incontinent of urine during the event. Following the episode, he was confused and drowsy for approximately 30 minutes before gradually returning to his baseline.

Upon interview, the patient recalls that just before the event he experienced an intense feeling of deja vu accompanied by a peculiar rising sensation from his epigastrium toward his throat. He describes having had similar brief episodes of deja vu with the epigastric rising sensation approximately three to four times over the past six months, each lasting 30 to 60 seconds, after which he would feel momentarily disoriented. He attributed these episodes to stress and did not seek medical attention. He denies any prior convulsions, loss of consciousness, or similar episodes in the past other than the febrile seizures in early childhood documented in his medical records. There has been no recent illness, fever, head trauma, sleep deprivation, or changes in medications. He denies any preceding headache, visual changes, or weakness. He has never taken antiepileptic medications.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz, symmetric, well-modulated alpha rhythm attenuating with eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves and sleep spindles observed during stage II sleep

findings.frequency: Frequent, occurring every 2-3 minutes during wakefulness and increasing during drowsiness
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F7 with dipole field extending to T3 and F3, duration 40-70 ms, followed by slow wave
findings.state: Wakefulness and drowsiness, activated during sleep

findings.frequency: Intermittent runs of 2-3 Hz rhythmic delta activity lasting 3-8 seconds

artifacts.impact: Minimal, did not obscure interictal abnormalities

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes performed without provocation of electrographic seizure activity; mild increase in background slowing noted, symmetric
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response, no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG due to frequent left anterior temporal sharp waves with phase reversal at F7 and intermittent left temporal rhythmic delta activity (TIRDA). No electrographic seizures were captured during this 40-minute routine recording. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 40-minute recording; no clinical events captured. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring recommended to capture habitual events.

findings.size: Left hippocampal volume reduced by approximately 25% compared to the right

signal_characteristics.T1: Decreased volume and loss of internal architecture of the left hippocampus
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in the left hippocampus relative to the right
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal in the left hippocampus, most pronounced in the body and tail

findings.borders: Well-defined volume loss without associated mass lesion

volumetrics.left_hippocampus: 2.1 cm3 (below 5th percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: No other cortical or subcortical signal abnormalities
Ventricles and sulci normal in caliber for patient age
mri.impression: Left mesial temporal sclerosis with significant hippocampal volume loss and T2/FLAIR hyperintensity. No mass lesion, acute infarction, or hemorrhage. Findings are concordant with a left temporal lobe epileptogenic focus. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm. No arrhythmia, conduction abnormality, or ischemic changes.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 154 mEq/L (H), Prolactin 38.9 ng/mL (H).

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture recorded over 72 hours of continuous monitoring

findings.frequency: Frequent, averaging 12 per hour during wakefulness, increasing to 25 per hour during NREM sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F7, maximum negativity at F7/T3, followed by after-going slow wave
findings.state: All states of consciousness, maximal during NREM sleep

findings.location: Left anterior temporal onset (F7/T3), evolving to left hemispheric
findings.frequency: Two clinical seizures captured over 72 hours
findings.morphology: Seizure onset with left temporal rhythmic 5-7 Hz theta activity evolving to 2-3 Hz delta with spread to left parasagittal region over 55 seconds, followed by bilateral involvement
findings.state: Both seizures occurred during wakefulness, one during quiet rest and one during conversation

findings.morphology: Rhythmic 2-3 Hz delta activity in left temporal chain

artifacts.impact: Obscured late ictal EEG during bilateral tonic-clonic phase but onset clearly captured

output.impression: Abnormal 72-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. Two habitual clinical seizures captured with clear left anterior temporal electrographic onset (F7/T3) concordant with clinical semiology of temporal lobe seizures (aura with epigastric rising, behavioral arrest, oral and manual automatisms, followed by focal to bilateral tonic-clonic activity in the second event). Frequent left anterior temporal interictal epileptiform discharges. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Movement artifact during bilateral tonic-clonic phase limited assessment of late ictal patterns in the second seizure.

findings.location: Left hippocampus, most prominent in the body and tail segments
findings.size: Left hippocampal volume 2.0 cm3 versus right 3.5 cm3, asymmetry ratio 0.27

signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Loss of normal internal structure of the left hippocampus with reduced gray-white differentiation
signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Markedly hyperintense signal in the left hippocampus on thin coronal cuts (3 mm slices perpendicular to the long axis of the hippocampus)
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Hyperintense signal in the left hippocampal body and tail, clearly abnormal compared to the right

findings.mass_effect: None. Mild ex vacuo dilation of the left temporal horn.
findings.borders: Well-defined atrophic changes without associated mass

signal_characteristics.overall: Normal signal throughout the remainder of the cerebral cortex, white matter, basal ganglia, thalami, brainstem, and cerebellum

additional_observations: No evidence of focal cortical dysplasia elsewhere
output.impression: No dual pathology or other structural abnormalities. Findings concordant with left temporal lobe epilepsy. Clinical correlation recommended.

Neuropsychological_Testing.test: Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Long Delay Free Recall)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Full Scale IQ (WAIS-IV) 112 standard score (H), Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Total Learning) T-score 38 T-score (H), Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Long Delay Free Recall) T-score 34 T-score (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Total Learning): T-score 38 T-score (L)
Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Long Delay Free Recall): T-score 34 T-score (H)

findings.location: Left mesial and lateral temporal lobe

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Markedly reduced FDG uptake in the left mesial temporal structures (hippocampus, parahippocampal gyrus, and amygdala) and moderately reduced uptake in the left lateral temporal neocortex compared to the right
signal_characteristics.asymmetry_index: Left-to-right temporal lobe asymmetry index 0.78 (normal >0.90), indicating significant left temporal hypometabolism

findings.location: Bilateral frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes; right temporal lobe; basal ganglia; thalami; cerebellum

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Symmetric and normal FDG uptake throughout the remainder of the brain

additional_observations: Interictal FDG-PET acquired during EEG-confirmed interictal state
No areas of hypermetabolism to suggest ictal activity during acquisition
output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates significant left mesial and lateral temporal hypometabolism concordant with left mesial temporal sclerosis on MRI and left temporal epileptiform activity on EEG. This multi-modal concordance strongly supports left temporal lobe epilepsy and is favorable for surgical candidacy. Clinical correlation recommended.

Antiepileptic_Drug_Levels.value: Not applicable - patient not yet on AEDs at time of initial presentation

output.query: temporal lobe epilepsy mesial temporal sclerosis treatment outcomes

results.title: Randomized controlled trial of early surgery for temporal lobe epilepsy (ERSET)
results.summary: Early surgical intervention for mesial temporal lobe epilepsy resulted in significantly better seizure freedom (73% vs 0%) compared to continued medical therapy at 2 years.

results.title: Long-term outcomes of temporal lobe epilepsy surgery
results.summary: Approximately 52% of patients remained seizure-free at 10 years following temporal lobe resection. Mesial temporal sclerosis was associated with favorable surgical outcomes.

results.title: ILAE consensus on definition of drug-resistant epilepsy
results.summary: Drug-resistant epilepsy defined as failure of adequate trials of two tolerated, appropriately chosen, and used AED schedules to achieve sustained seizure freedom.

output.summary: Current evidence supports early referral for epilepsy surgery evaluation in patients with mesial temporal sclerosis and concordant EEG findings. The ERSET trial demonstrated superior outcomes with early surgery compared to prolonged medical therapy. Approximately 60-80% of patients achieve seizure freedom after anterior temporal lobectomy for MTS. First-line AED options for focal epilepsy include levetiracetam, lamotrigine, and oxcarbazepine.

output.proposed: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily (titrate to 1000 mg twice daily)
warnings: Psychiatric adverse effects (irritability, mood changes, behavioral disturbance) in approximately 10-15% of patients
Dose adjustment needed in renal impairment (not applicable in this patient with normal creatinine)
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily, titrate slowly to 200 mg daily (slower titration but favorable side effect profile)
Oxcarbazepine 300 mg twice daily, titrate to 600 mg twice daily
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily, titrate to 200 mg twice daily

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (left mesial temporal lobe epilepsy with hippocampal sclerosis)

differential.key_features: Witnessed semiology is classic for temporal lobe epilepsy with typical aura, automatisms, and secondary generalization. Elevated post-ictal prolactin and clear EEG abnormalities argue against PNES.

No prodrome of lightheadedness or presyncope. Prolonged post-ictal confusion inconsistent with syncope. Normal ECG. Witnessed tonic-clonic activity lasted >60 seconds, far longer than convulsive syncope.

Aura in migraine spreads slowly over 5-20 minutes and is typically visual or sensory. Patient's episodes are brief (seconds), include deja vu and epigastric rising (classic temporal lobe aura), and are followed by altered awareness.

Patient is young with no vascular risk factors. TIAs typically cause negative symptoms (weakness, numbness, aphasia), not the positive phenomena (deja vu, epigastric rising) and altered awareness seen here.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history including seizure semiology, aura description, witnesses account, and duration of post-ictal period
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Classic temporal lobe aura (deja vu, epigastric rising) followed by focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure with post-ictal confusion
Normal interictal neurological exam with evidence of recent tongue bite
Left anterior temporal sharp waves and TIRDA consistent with left temporal epileptogenic focus

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain with epilepsy protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left hippocampal sclerosis with volume loss and T2/FLAIR hyperintensity

optimal_actions.action: Obtain basic labs including BMP, CBC, prolactin, and urine drug screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic panel, elevated post-ictal prolactin, negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to rule out cardiac etiology
Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy with levetiracetam or lamotrigine
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant drug interactions, appropriate first-line AED choice for focal epilepsy

optimal_actions.action: Counsel patient on driving restrictions and seizure safety precautions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient informed of state driving laws regarding seizures, need to avoid heights and swimming alone

optimal_actions.action: Refer to comprehensive epilepsy center for surgical evaluation given structural lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Referral placed for epilepsy surgery workup

critical_actions: Obtain EEG to characterize epileptiform activity and lateralize the epileptogenic focus
Obtain brain MRI to identify structural etiology (hippocampal sclerosis)
Obtain basic metabolic panel and CBC to exclude metabolic causes of seizure
Initiate appropriate antiepileptic drug therapy for focal epilepsy
Counsel patient on driving restrictions and seizure safety precautions
Assess for and document seizure semiology and aura to support localization
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing carbamazepine or phenytoin without checking for drug interactions and monitoring levels (not contraindicated per se but require more monitoring than first-line agents)
Performing lumbar puncture in this clinical scenario (no indication for CSF analysis; no signs of infection or inflammation)
Discharging patient without initiating antiepileptic therapy after a focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure with clear epileptogenic lesion
Prescribing bupropion which lowers seizure threshold
Advising that the patient can continue driving immediately
key_reasoning_points: The combination of deja vu and epigastric rising sensation is a classic temporal lobe aura, strongly localizing the seizure onset to the mesial temporal structures
The prior brief episodes of deja vu with disorientation represent focal aware seizures (previously called simple partial seizures) that were unrecognized, indicating this is not truly a first seizure
History of febrile seizures in childhood is a well-established risk factor for mesial temporal sclerosis and subsequent temporal lobe epilepsy
Concordance across modalities (semiology, EEG lateralization, MRI lesion) is essential for accurate localization and is particularly important when considering epilepsy surgery
Elevated post-ictal prolactin supports the diagnosis of an epileptic seizure versus a psychogenic event, though it must be drawn within 10-20 minutes for maximal sensitivity

Left mesial temporal sclerosis with concordant left temporal EEG findings and material-specific verbal memory deficit makes this patient a potential surgical candidate if seizures prove medically refractory

metadata.estimated_complexity: Straightforward - classic presentation with concordant findings
